content:
# Luke Chapter 13 — The PNEUMA Bible (Dynamic)
**Translator:** PNEUMA | **Source:** Codex Sinaiticus (Aleph, 01), PDF pages 104-105 | **Source Image:** source-texts/greek-NT/LUK_013_page_001.jpg, LUK_013_page_002.jpg

---

**1** At that very time, some people came and told Jesus about the Galileans whose blood Pilate had mixed with their sacrifices. **2** He answered them, "Do you think those Galileans were worse sinners than all the other Galileans because they suffered this way? **3** Not at all! But I tell you, unless you turn your lives around, you will all perish just as they did. **4** Or what about those eighteen people who were killed when the tower at Siloam collapsed on them — do you think they were guiltier than everyone else living in Jerusalem? **5** Not at all! But I tell you, unless you turn your lives around, you will all perish in the same way."

**6** Then he told them this parable: "A man had a fig tree planted in his vineyard. He went to look for fruit on it and found none. **7** So he said to the vineyard keeper, 'Look, I have been coming to this fig tree for three years expecting to find fruit, and there has been nothing. Cut it down! Why should it go on wasting the soil?' **8** But the keeper replied, 'Sir, leave it alone for one more year. Let me dig around it and put manure on it. **9** If it produces fruit next year, wonderful. But if not, then cut it down.'"

**10** One Sabbath, Jesus was teaching in one of the synagogues. **11** A woman was there who had been crippled by a spirit for eighteen years. She was bent double and completely unable to stand up straight. **12** When Jesus saw her, he called her forward and said to her, "Woman, you are set free from your affliction!" **13** Then he placed his hands on her, and immediately she straightened up and began praising God.

**14** But the synagogue leader was indignant because Jesus had healed on the Sabbath. He said to the crowd, "There are six days for work. Come and be healed on those days, not on the Sabbath!" **15** The Lord answered him, "You hypocrites! Does not each of you untie his ox or donkey from the feeding trough on the Sabbath and lead it out to give it water? **16** Then should not this woman — a daughter of Abraham whom Satan has kept bound for eighteen long years — be released from her chains on the Sabbath day?" **17** When he said this, all his opponents were humiliated, and the entire crowd was overjoyed at all the wonderful things he was doing.

**18** Then Jesus said, "What is the kingdom of God like? What can I compare it to? **19** It is like a mustard seed that someone took and planted in a garden. It grew and became a tree, and the birds of the sky nested in its branches."

**20** Again he said, "What can I compare the kingdom of God to? **21** It is like yeast that a woman took and mixed into a large amount of flour until it worked all the way through the dough."

**22** He continued on his way toward Jerusalem, teaching in one town and village after another. **23** Someone asked him, "Lord, are only a few people going to be saved?" He said to them, **24** "Make every effort to enter through the narrow door, because many, I tell you, will try to get in and will not be able to. **25** Once the master of the house gets up and shuts the door, you will be left standing outside knocking and pleading, 'Lord, open the door for us!' But he will reply, 'I do not know you or where you come from.' **26** Then you will say, 'We ate and drank with you, and you taught in our streets!' **27** But he will say, 'I do not know where you come from. Get away from me, all you who practice evil!'

**28** "There will be weeping and grinding of teeth when you see Abraham, Isaac, Jacob, and all the prophets in the kingdom of God, but you yourselves thrown out. **29** People will come from east and west, from north and south, and take their places at the feast in the kingdom of God. **30** And indeed, some who are last will be first, and some who are first will be last."

**31** At that time, some Pharisees came to him and said, "Get out of here! Leave this place, because Herod wants to kill you." **32** He replied, "Go tell that fox: 'Look, I am driving out demons and performing healings today and tomorrow, and on the third day I will reach my goal.' **33** But I must keep moving today, tomorrow, and the next day — for it would never do for a prophet to be killed outside Jerusalem!

**34** "Jerusalem, Jerusalem, you who kill the prophets and stone those sent to you! How often I have longed to gather your children together, as a hen gathers her chicks under her wings, but you were not willing! **35** Look — your house is left to you desolate. I tell you, you will not see me until the time comes when you say, 'Blessed is the one who comes in the name of the Lord!'"

---

## PNEUMA's Meaning Notes

- **13:1 — "the Galileans whose blood Pilate had mixed with their sacrifices"**: This incident is not recorded in any other historical source, though it fits Pilate's known character. Josephus documents multiple occasions when Pilate used lethal force against Jewish crowds. The horror here is specific: these Galileans were slaughtered while offering sacrifices at the temple — their own blood literally mingling with the blood of their animal offerings on the altar. It was a desecration of the most sacred act in Jewish worship. The people who report this to Jesus may expect him to pronounce theological judgment on the victims (they must have deserved it) or political judgment on Pilate (he is a monster). Jesus refuses both responses.

- **13:2-5 — "unless you turn your lives around, you will all perish"**: The Greek METANOHTE (metanoeite) means a complete transformation of mind and direction — not merely feeling sorry but fundamentally reorienting one's life. Jesus demolishes the popular theology that suffering is proportional to sinfulness. The Galilean victims were not worse sinners; the eighteen crushed at Siloam were not guiltier than their neighbors. But Jesus does not stop at correcting bad theology — he pivots to an urgent demand. The real question is not "why did they die?" but "are you ready if you do?" Both disasters become warnings, not explanations. The repetition of the warning twice — with two different examples — underscores its urgency.

- **13:4 — "the tower at Siloam"**: Siloam was in the southeastern part of Jerusalem, near the Pool of Siloam. The tower collapse appears to have been a construction accident, not an act of war or divine punishment — which is precisely Jesus' point. Some deaths are caused by political violence (Pilate's massacre), others by random structural failure (the falling tower). Neither reveals anything about the victims' moral standing. Jesus refuses to let his audience play theologian with other people's tragedies while ignoring the condition of their own hearts.

- **13:6-9 — The parable of the barren fig tree**: The fig tree planted in a vineyard was common practice in first-century Palestine — fig trees were often interplanted among grapevines. The owner's three-year wait is significant: fig trees typically take three years after planting to begin bearing fruit, so this tree has had more than enough time. The vineyard keeper's request for one more year of intensive care — digging and fertilizing — represents a last-chance intervention. The parable sits between the call to repentance (vv. 1-5) and the healing miracles that follow, embodying the tension between judgment and mercy. God is patient, but patience has a horizon. The unnamed outcome — will the tree produce? — leaves the decision with the audience.

- **13:10-11 — "crippled by a spirit for eighteen years"**: The Greek PNEUMA ASTHENEIAS (pneuma astheneias) literally means "a spirit of weakness" or "a spirit of infirmity." Luke, the physician-evangelist, describes both the spiritual cause and the physical symptom: she was SYNKYPTOUSA (synkyptousa, "bent together" or "doubled over") and completely unable (ME DYNAMENE, me dynamene) to straighten herself. The eighteen years matches the eighteen victims at Siloam (v. 4) — possibly a deliberate literary echo. Where those eighteen died without warning, this woman has endured a living death of disability for the same duration. Jesus will give her what the tower victims never received: restoration.

- **13:12 — "Woman, you are set free from your affliction"**: Jesus does not say "be healed" but APOLELYSAI (apolelysai, "you have been released") — the same word used for releasing prisoners or freeing slaves. The perfect tense indicates that the liberation is already accomplished the moment he speaks. He reframes her condition from a medical problem to a bondage problem, and his word is the emancipation decree. The combination of word (v. 12) and touch (v. 13) is characteristic of Luke's healing narratives.

- **13:14 — "the synagogue leader was indignant"**: The leader does not address Jesus directly — he speaks to the crowd, telling them to come for healing on working days. This is both cowardice (avoiding a direct confrontation with Jesus) and cruelty (blaming the sick for coming on the wrong day, as if they had scheduled their own deliverance). He treats healing as "work" prohibited on the Sabbath, reducing a divine act of liberation to a labor regulation.

- **13:15-16 — "a daughter of Abraham whom Satan has kept bound"**: Jesus' argument is devastating in its logic. The Pharisees themselves untied (LYEI, lyei) their animals on the Sabbath for basic care. Should not this woman — not an animal but a DAUGHTER OF ABRAHAM, a full member of the covenant community — be untied (LYTHENAI, lythenai) from Satan's binding? The same root word (lyo, "to loose/untie") connects the animal to the woman, making the comparison inescapable. If Sabbath law permits untying a donkey for water, it certainly permits untying a human being from eighteen years of satanic bondage. Jesus redefines the Sabbath not as a day of restricted activity but as the most appropriate day imaginable for liberation — the day God rests by setting the oppressed free.

- **13:18-19 — The mustard seed**: The KOKKON SINAPEŌS (kokkon sinapeōs, "grain of mustard") was proverbially the smallest seed known in Palestinian agriculture. Yet the mustard plant (Brassica nigra) could grow to ten or twelve feet — large enough for birds to perch in. The image of birds nesting in its branches echoes Daniel 4:12 and Ezekiel 17:23, where a great tree sheltering birds represents a mighty kingdom providing refuge for the nations. The kingdom of God begins as something absurdly small and becomes something that shelters the world. The Sinaiticus text says he threw it EIS KHPON (eis kepon, "into a garden"), a distinctly Lukan detail.

- **13:20-21 — Yeast worked through the dough**: The ZYMĒ (zyme, "yeast" or "leaven") parable complements the mustard seed. Where the seed illustrates visible, external growth, the yeast illustrates invisible, internal transformation. A woman hides (EKRYPSEN, ekrypsen — literally "concealed") the yeast in three measures (SATA TRIA, sata tria) of flour — an enormous quantity, roughly fifty pounds, enough to feed well over a hundred people. The kingdom does not merely grow; it permeates. It works from within, quietly and invisibly, until everything is transformed. Note that in 12:1, yeast represented the corrupting influence of the Pharisees' hypocrisy. Here it represents the transforming influence of God's kingdom — the same image repurposed with the opposite meaning.

- **13:22 — "He continued on his way toward Jerusalem"**: Luke reminds the reader of the great journey motif that has structured the narrative since 9:51 ("he set his face toward Jerusalem"). Every teaching, healing, and encounter in this section takes place under the shadow of Jesus' determined march toward the city where he will die. This geographical note is not incidental — it colors everything that follows, especially the lament over Jerusalem at the chapter's end.

- **13:23-24 — "Lord, are only a few going to be saved?"**: The question reflects a common rabbinic debate about the scope of salvation. Jesus refuses to answer with a number. Instead of satisfying theological curiosity, he issues a command: AGŌNIZESTHE (agonizesthe, "struggle" or "strive") — a word from the athletic arena, suggesting the intense effort of a competitor in a contest. The narrow door (STENES THYRAS, stenes thyras) is not about elite exclusivity but about the demanding nature of genuine discipleship. Many will "seek" (ZĒTĒSOUSIN) to enter but will not be able — not because God is stingy but because they seek on their own terms, too late, or without the radical commitment that passage requires.

- **13:25-27 — "'We ate and drank with you'"**: The excluded people protest their familiarity with Jesus — they shared meals with him, they heard him teach in their streets. But proximity is not the same as relationship. Having Jesus as a dinner guest or a visiting preacher does not constitute discipleship. The master's repeated "I do not know where you come from" (OUK OIDA YMAS POTHEN ESTE) is not a claim of ignorance but a declaration of non-recognition. They were never his. The phrase ERGATAI ADIKIAS ("workers of unrighteousness" or "practitioners of evil") reveals that the real issue is not knowledge or access but the pattern of one's life.

- **13:28-29 — "People will come from east and west"**: The weeping and grinding of teeth express the anguish of those who assumed their place at the table was guaranteed — they will see the patriarchs inside and themselves outside. The reversal is stunning: Gentiles from the four points of the compass will recline at the messianic banquet alongside Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob, while some of Abraham's biological descendants will be excluded. This was explosive teaching in a Jewish context. Covenant membership does not automatically secure a seat at the feast; genuine repentance and faith do.

- **13:30 — "some who are last will be first"**: This proverb capsulizes the great reversal that runs through all of Luke's Gospel. The outsiders become insiders; the insiders become outsiders. Those who assumed priority — by birth, ethnicity, religious standing, or social position — discover they have none, while those who had no claim at all are welcomed first. This is not arbitrary divine caprice but the natural consequence of a kingdom where the currency is humility, repentance, and trust rather than status and entitlement.

- **13:31 — "some Pharisees came... 'Herod wants to kill you'"**: This is one of the rare moments when Pharisees appear sympathetic — or at least useful. Their motives are debated: are they genuinely warning Jesus out of concern, or are they acting as Herod's agents, trying to frighten him out of the territory? Either way, Jesus is unmoved. Herod Antipas, who had already beheaded John the Baptist, now sets his sights on Jesus. The political threat is real, but Jesus subordinates it entirely to his divine mission.

- **13:32 — "Go tell that fox"**: ALŌPEKI (alopeki, "fox") was not a compliment. In Jewish culture, the fox represented craftiness, insignificance, and destructiveness — a sly but ultimately minor predator, in contrast to the lion (a symbol of true royalty). By calling Herod a fox, Jesus simultaneously acknowledges his cunning and dismisses his importance. The phrase "on the third day I will reach my goal" (TELEIOUMAI, teleioumai — "I am perfected" or "I am completed") is loaded with double meaning. Immediately, it refers to the completion of his ministry of healing and exorcism. But for Luke's readers, "the third day" inevitably points to the resurrection — the ultimate completion of Jesus' mission.

- **13:33 — "it would never do for a prophet to be killed outside Jerusalem"**: This is bitter irony. Jerusalem, the holy city, the place of God's dwelling, has such a consistent record of killing prophets that it has become unthinkable for a prophet to die anywhere else. The city that should be the safest place for God's messengers is historically the most dangerous. Jesus is not fatalistic — he is deliberately, knowingly walking into the place that will kill him, because that is where prophets go to die, and he is the prophet to end all prophets.

- **13:34 — "as a hen gathers her chicks under her wings"**: The image of God as a mother bird sheltering her young has deep Old Testament roots (Deuteronomy 32:11, Ruth 2:12, Psalms 17:8, 36:7, 91:4). Jesus applies this maternal image to himself — he is the hen who longs to protect Jerusalem's children from the coming destruction. The word ETHELĒSA (ethelesa, "I longed" or "I wanted") is in the aorist — pointing to repeated past attempts. POSAKIS (posakis, "how many times") reinforces the pattern: this is not a single rejected offer but a history of refused protection. The devastating final clause — KAI OUK ĒTHELĒSATE ("but you were not willing") — places the blame squarely on Jerusalem's refusal, not on any lack of divine desire. God's will to save was met by human will to resist.

- **13:35 — "your house is left to you desolate"**: OIKOS (oikos, "house") almost certainly refers to the temple — the house of God that is now abandoned by God's presence and left to those who rejected his messengers. The language echoes Jeremiah 22:5 and 12:7, where God abandons his "house" because of Israel's unfaithfulness. The final quotation — "Blessed is the one who comes in the name of the Lord" — is from Psalm 118:26, the great pilgrim psalm. Jesus implies that Jerusalem will not see him again until they are ready to welcome him with this cry. Whether this refers to the triumphal entry (Luke 19:38), the second coming, or a future national repentance remains one of the great interpretive questions of the passage. The chapter that began with a call to repentance ends with a conditional promise: recognition of Jesus is still possible, but only on his terms, and at his timing.
